interactions.summary: Jiang maps the National Assembly's internal factional splits: Jacobins divide into bourgeois conservatives, Girondin war speculators, Cordeliers seeking fuller proletarian revolution, and anti-Catholic radicals.
He introduces Robespierre as a petit-bourgeois provincial lawyer whose virtue, labor, Rousseauian conviction, and force of ideas make him de facto leader and lead to the Reign of Terror.

claims.claim: The French Revolution's internal factions reflect conflicting economic interests inside the middle class, not a unified people moving with one interest.
claims.temporal_scope: Reading of the revolutionary factional landscape.

claims.claim: Robespierre's authority comes less from formal dictatorship than from conviction, virtue, work, and Rousseauian clarity inside a collapsing revolutionary field.
Jiang reads the Reign of Terror as a policy of mass execution justified as revolutionary purification against internal enemies, speculation, hoarding, and conspiracy.
claims.temporal_scope: Description of the Terror in the lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Robespierre is almost a nerd, but he becomes dangerous because he believes the revolution must win if he works hard enough.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image distinguishes charismatic dictatorship from obsessive prophetic conviction.

signature_moments.moment: The Terror is recoded as human sacrifice, not only as policy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It's the idea of human sacrifice
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This turn opens the lecture's deeper mythic explanation of revolutionary violence.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The lower-class proletarian force of the revolution, named as those without expensive breeches or silk socks.
Robespierre's radical policy of mass execution meant to solidify and advance the revolution through terror.

chronology_notes.note: The transcript says '1990, 1991' in the revolutionary crisis setup; context indicates 1790-1791.

uncertainty_notes.note: Several faction names are distorted by ASR; interpretation relies on context and conventional French Revolution terms.
